title: An Ode On The Popular Superstitions Of The Highlands Of Scotland, Considered As The Subject Of Poetry
content:
Home, thou returnï¿½st from Thames, whose naiads long
Have seen thee lingï¿½ring with a fond delay
ï¿½Mid those soft friends, whose hearts, some future day,
Shall melt, perhaps, to hear thy tragic song.
Go, not unmindful of that cordial youth
Whom, long endearï¿½d, thou leavï¿½st by Lavantï¿½s side;
Together let us wish him lasting truth,
And joy untainted, with his destinï¿½d bride.
Go! nor regardless, while these numbers boast
My short-livï¿½d bliss, forget my social name;
But think far off how, on the southern coast,
I met thy friendship with an equal flame!
Fresh to that soil thou turnï¿½st, whose evï¿½ry vale
Shall prompt the poet, and his song demand:
To thee thy copious subjects neï¿½er shall fail;
Thou needï¿½st but take the pencil to thy hand,
And paint what all believe who own thy genial land.

There must thou wake perforce thy Doric quill;
ï¿½Tis Fancyï¿½s land to which thou settï¿½st thy feet;
Where still, ï¿½tis said, the fairy people meet,
Beneath each birken shade, on mead or hill.
There, each trim lass that skims the milky store
To the swart tribes their creamy bowl allots;
By night they sip it round the cottage door,
While airy minstrels warble jocund notes.
There every herd, by sad experience, knows
How, wingï¿½d with fate, their elf-shot arrows fly,
When the sick ewe her summer food forgoes,
Or, stretchï¿½d on earth, the heart-smit heifers lie.
Such airy beings awe thï¿½ untutorï¿½d swain:
Nor thou, though learnï¿½d, his homelier thoughts neglect;
Let thy sweet muse the rural faith sustain;
These are the themes of simple, sure effect,
That add new conquests to her boundless reign.
And fill, with double force, her heart-commanding strain.

Evï¿½n yet preservï¿½d, how often mayï¿½st thou hear,
Where to the pole the Boreal mountains run,
Taught by the father to his listï¿½ning son,
Strange lays, whose power had charmï¿½d a Spenserï¿½s ear.
At evï¿½ry pause, before thy mind possest,
Old Runic bards shall seem to rise around,
With uncouth lyres, in many-coloured vest,
Their matted hair with boughs fantastic crownï¿½d:
Whether thou biddï¿½st the well-taught hind repeat
The choral dirge that mourns some chieftain brave,
When evï¿½ry shrieking maid her bosom beat,
And strewï¿½d with choicest herbs his scented grave;
Or whether, sitting in the shepherdï¿½s shiel,
Thou hearï¿½st some sounding tale of warï¿½s alarms;
When at the bugleï¿½s call, with fire and steel,
The sturdy clans pourï¿½d forth their bony swarms,
And hostile brothers met to prove each otherï¿½s arms.

ï¿½Tis thine to sing, how, framing hideous spells,
In Skyï¿½s lone isle, the gifted wizard seer,
Lodged in the wintry cave with [fateï¿½s fell spear,]
Or in the depth of Uistï¿½s dark forest dwells:
How they, whose sight such dreary dreams engross,
With their own visions oft astonishï¿½d droop.
When, oï¿½er the watï¿½ry strath, or quaggy moss,
They see the gliding ghosts unbodied troop.
Or, if in sports, or on the festive green,
Their [piercing] glance some fated youth descry,
Who now, perhaps, in lusty vigour seen,
And rosy health, shall soon lamented die.
For them the viewless forms of air obey;
Their bidding heed, and at their beck repair:
They know what spirit brews the stormful day,
And heartless, oft like moody madness, stare
To see the phantom train their secret work prepare.

[Twenty-five lines in this section are missing from available
 manuscripts]

What though far off, from some dark dell espied,
His glimmï¿½ring mazes cheer thï¿½ excursive sight,
Yet turn, ye wandï¿½rers, turn your steps aside,
8Nor trust the guidance of that faithless light;
For watchful, lurking, ï¿½mid thï¿½ unrustling reed,
At those mirk hours the wily monster lies,
And listens oft to hear the passing steed,
And frequent round him rolls his sullen eyes,
If chance his savage wrath may some weak wretch surprise.

Ah, luckless swain, oï¿½er all unblest indeed!
Whom late bewilderï¿½d in the dank, dark fen,
Far from his flocks and smoking hamlet then!
To that sad spot [his wayward fate shall lead]:
On him, enragï¿½d, the fiend in angry mood,
Shall never look with pityï¿½s kind concern,
But instant, furious, raise the whelming flood
Oï¿½er its drownï¿½d bank, forbidding all return.
Or, if he meditate his wishï¿½d escape,
To some dim hill that seems uprising near,
To his faint eye the grim and grisly shape,
In all its terrors clad, shall wild appear.
Meantime the watï¿½ry surge shall round him rise,
Pourï¿½d sudden forth from evï¿½ry swelling source.
What now remains but tears and hopeless sighs?
His fear-shook limbs have lost their youthly force,
And down the waves he floats, a pale and breathless, corse.

For him in vain his anxious wife shall wait,
Or wander forth to meet him on his way;
For him in vain at to-fall of the day,
His babes shall linger at thï¿½ unclosing gate.
Ah, neï¿½er shall he return! Alone, if night
Her travellï¿½d limbs in broken slumbers steep,
With drooping willows dressï¿½d, his mournful sprite
Shall visit sad, perchance, her silent sleep:
Then he, perhaps, with moist and watï¿½ry hand,
Shall fondly seem to press her shuddï¿½ring cheek,
And with his blue-swoln face before her stand,
And, shivï¿½ring cold, these piteous accents speak:
ï¿½Pursue, dear wife, thy daily toils pursue,
At dawn or dusk, industrious as before;
Nor eï¿½er of me one hapless thought renew,
While I lie weltï¿½ring on the osierï¿½d shore,
Drownï¿½d by the kelpieï¿½s wrath, nor eï¿½er shall aid thee more!ï¿½

Unbounded is thy range; with varied style
Thy Muse may, like those feathï¿½ry tribes which spring
From their rude rocks, extend her skirting wing
Round the moist marge of each cold Hebrid isle.
To that hoar pile, which still its ruin shows:
In whose small vaults a pigmy-folk is found,
Whose bones the delver with his spade upthrows,
And culls them, wondï¿½ring, from the hallowï¿½d ground!
Or thither, where, beneath the showï¿½ry west,
The mighty kings of three fair realms are laid:
Once foes, perhaps, together now they rest,
No slaves revere them, and no wars invade:
Yet frequent now, at midnightï¿½s solemn hour,
The rifted mounds their yawning cells unfold,
And forth the monarchs stalk with sovï¿½reign powï¿½r,
In pageant robes, and wreathï¿½d with sheeny gold,
And on their twilight tombs aerial council hold.

But, O! oï¿½er all, forget not Kildaï¿½s race,
On whose bleak rocks, which brave the wasting tides,
Fair Natureï¿½s daughter, Virtue, yet abides.
Go, just, as they, their blameless manners trace!
Then to my ear transmit some gentle song,
Of those whose lives are yet sincere and plain,
Their bounded walks the rugged cliffs along,
And all their prospect but the wintry main.
With sparing tempï¿½rance, at the needful time,
They drain the sainted spring; or, hunger-prest,
Along thï¿½ Atlantic rock undreading climb,
And of its eggs despoil the solanï¿½s nest.
Thus blest in primal innocence, they live,
Sufficï¿½d and happy with that frugal fare
Which tasteful toil and hourly danger give.
Hard is their shallow soil, and bleak and bare;
Nor ever vernal bee was heard to murmur there!

Nor needï¿½st thou blush that such false themes engage
Thy gentle mind, of fairer stores possest;
For not alone they touch the village breast,
But fillï¿½d in elder time thï¿½ historic page.
There Shakespeareï¿½s self, with evï¿½ry garland crownï¿½d,
[Flew to those fairy climes his fancy sheen],
In musing hour, his wayward sisters found,
And with their terrors drest the magic scene.
From them he sung, when ï¿½mid his bold design,
Before the Scot afflicted and aghast,
The shadowy kings of Banquoï¿½s fated line
Through the dark cave in gleamy pageant passï¿½d.
Proceed, nor quit the tales which, simply told
Could once so well my answï¿½ring bosom pierce;
Proceed, in forceful sounds, and colours bold,
The native legends of thy land rehearse;
To such adapt thy lyre and suit thy powerful verse.

In scenes like these, which, daring to depart
From sober truth, are still to nature true,
And call forth fresh delight to Fancyï¿½s view,
Thï¿½ heroic muse employï¿½d her Tassoï¿½s art!
How have I trembled, when, at Tancredï¿½s stroke,
Its gushing blood the gaping cypress pourï¿½d;
When each live plant with mortal accents spoke,
And the wild blast upheavï¿½d the vanishï¿½d sword!
How have I sat, when pipï¿½d the pensive wind,
To hear his harp by British Fairfax strung;
Prevailing poet! whose undoubting mind
Believï¿½d the magic wonders which he sung!
Hence, at each sound, imagination glows;
[Hence, at each picture, vivid life starts here!]
Hence his warm lay with softest sweetness flows;
Melting its flows, pure, numï¿½rous, strong, and clear,
And fills thï¿½ impassionï¿½d heart, and wins the harmonious ear!

All hail, ye scenes that oï¿½er my soul prevail!
Ye [spacious] friths and lakes, which, far away,
Are by smooth Annan fillï¿½d or pastï¿½ral Tay,
Or Donï¿½s romantic springs at distance, hail!
The time shall come when I, perhaps, may tread
Your lowly glens, oï¿½erhung with spreading broom;
Or, oï¿½er your stretching heaths, by fancy led;
[Or oï¿½er your mountains creep, in awful gloom!]
Then will I dress once more the faded bowï¿½r,
Where Jonson sat in Drummondï¿½s [classic] shade;
Or crop, from Tiviotï¿½s dale, each [lyric flower,]
And mourn, on Yarrowï¿½s banks, [where Willyï¿½s laid!]
Meantime, ye Powï¿½rs, that on the plains which bore
The cordial youth, on Lothianï¿½s plains, attend,
Whereï¿½er he dwell, on hill, or lowly muir,
To him I lose, your kind protection lend,
And, touchï¿½d with love like mine, preserve my absent friend!
meaning:
About: William Collins (25 December 1721 ï¿½ 12 June 1759), English poet

Second in influence only to Thomas Gray, he was an important poet of the middle decades of the 18th century. His lyrical odes mark a turn away from the Augustan poetry of Alexander Pope's generation and towards the romantic era which would soon follow.
